speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two. March 24, 1996. My name is Susan Peirez speaking with Eli Stern. Your-- you wanted to talk about your-- you wanted to mention your relatives, your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I mentioned earlier, my father is-- was one of 10 children. My father was named Mordechai Hersh. His brother was name Ezriel. He had a sister, Sarah, Leah, Rochel, Deborah, Tobe, Eke, and [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE], his name is Abe Stern. He lives in Montreal, Canada today. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother was one of four. My mother's name, as I said, was Leah. She had a sister, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] is [INAUDIBLE]. He's a survivor as well. And he lives in Los Angeles, California today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being with all these family members during the holiday together, the Shabbas?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember coming to grandma for my-- father's mother was living close to us. And we practically lived in her house. And we would always get together on Yontif and on Shabbas. It was a big treat to us to-- on the Jewish holiday, to go visit my mother's family, which was in another town. And there, we were guests more so, because we didn't live there. And we didn't see each other that often. So it was more of a treat for me to come and visit over there. Because they made a big to-do about the children there when we came to visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of to do would they make for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would give us candy or-- or little treats. Or-- they gave us more privileges than mother would.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like if mother would tell us of certain things that we can't do, we knew that we can get away with it if grandma-- if we visit grandma in the next town, in Petrova, that mother came from. So we were always looking forward to-- to visit that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during the war, when the Hungarians came in, you were speaking about Shabbas. The-- the deportations would usually take place on Shabbas. Were you still able to- to observe holidays during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Life always went on. No matter how bad it got, people never gave up on going to the synagogue. Because as a matter of fact, when things got worse, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we went to the synagogue. And there were special prayers and a [INAUDIBLE] during bad times. And we prayed for things to get better. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Obviously, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things got worse. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There was, again, on the Shabbas, we got orders to assemble in the synagogue. Every family, every Jewish family in Stramtura, in that town that I lived in, had to assemble on the Shabbas in the synagogue. And the only-- you could only carry with you whatever you can carry on your-- on your back. You couldn't take any-- any more than you can carry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We all gathered in the synagogue and in the backyard of the synagogue, because there was 80 some families with children and so forth. So we all gathered there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you carry?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- gave-- usually, if you were bigger, you carried a little bit more. Smaller people carried 7 less. Whatever-- people grabbed whatever they can. They grabbed pillows.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember grabbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't grab. I was given to carry something by my parents. I don't remember what was-- I carried a valise. But I don't know what was in it. My parents said, you carry that. And you-- you watch that. That's your responsibility to watch out for. We couldn't really carry-- take with us, like I said, more than what we were able to carry with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who were you-- who were you with? What family members were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point we were together. The whole family was intact, my brother, Averum Maier, my brother, Yitzhak, my sister, Tobi, and my little brother, Baruch, and my parents, and my grandmother. And at that point, we were all together. The whole town is still-- everybody is still together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We see a line of horse-driven carriages, horse-drawn carriages lining up. And we're ordered to board these horse-driven carriages. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we're lined up. It's a long line of horse and buggies taking us. We still don't know where we were going. We're passing other villages. And we get to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a place called Dragomiresti. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the ghetto. They assembled other towns and villages into that place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It got so tight because so many villages-- the-- the Jewish families from all these villages were pushed into this one village that 40, 50, and 60 people would live in one house. They made use of the attic, the basement. If people had a barn, people were put up in barns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of year you arrived there with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, I'll guess, the beginning of May, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go? Where did you all stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wound up in a house that I didn't know anybody. But we were just put together with other people that I've never seen before. But there was no choice in the matter. And we just had to make the best that we could. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first thing my mother and father did was try to make us as comfortable as possible. And we didn't have the comforts, naturally, of the house that we just left without even being able to close the door on the house. We weren't even able to lock the door on that house when we left that house. And they-- we are in-- now, we're in the ghetto in Dragomiresti. We are still guarded and watched by the Hungarian gendarmes, the feathers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They tell us-- they come out. And they form what they call a Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH]. There were Jewish police without any power of a regular policeman, but the-- they were called the Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH]. They had yellow armbands where we all had to wear the yellow star. And they would be the ones to carry out the gendarmes' orders. If any new order was given, the Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH] used to go down from house to house and tell people whatever order it was of that day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of the orders that I can remember was [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] young men 16 years and older [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] have-- have to assemble at a certain point. And they were giving picks and shovels and other tools of work. And they took them out to do labor work. I-- I remember father saying the labor work that these people were doing wasn't really of any consequence. They just wanted to wear the people down, keep them busy from not thinking. They knew what was ahead, but we did not. We still didn't know. We didn't hear any news at this point from any other ghettos to know what-- what-- what to expect.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the place that you were living in?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 50 or 60 people in that house. It was crowded. But we got to live with it. We learned to live with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- because we were still together. My mother and father and my siblings were still together. And as long as we were together, we somehow learned to live with it. We always-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we always used to say, it could be worse. It could get worse. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't-- as I say, we did not know what was in store for us. Rumors were that they will take us all into-- deep into Hungary. The men will be working in an industrial area. And they will let us stay together with our families. These were rumors. And maybe some of us wanted to believe them. Because none of us had any idea what is in store for us up ahead. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were in-- in the ghetto only a short time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] four to six weeks. I'm not sure about that, but a few-- several weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH] started to run around from house to house with a new edict. This time, it was everybody 14 years. All males, 14 years and up, can only take what they can carry. And they have to assemble in the local public school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] said goodbye from our mothers, and siblings, and grandparents. And we did what we were asked to do. We did assemble over in that public school. Some of us that were not as-- not too lucky-- there were a line of soldiers with fixed bayonets and some local civilian gentiles that were just standing there on both sides. There was a little foot bridge. We had to cross a little foot bridge to get across to get to the school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of us, as we crossed that little foot bridge, between two lines of guards and local hooligans-- were-- they had sticks. And people started to get beaten and hit with the rifle butts. And where that stick fell, it could have-- it could fall just any place, on your head, on your back, on your legs. There was no-- there was nobody that came to anybody's help. If you fell down, you got hit even more so. People just ran as fast as they could across. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- the old public school, before they assembled us over there, was used by-- by the army as stables for their horses. When we all had to run into the-- to the-- to that stable, to that public school that was turned into the stable, there was all kinds of horse manure and all kinds of dirt on the floor. But you just had to run in and do what you're told. Or, you knew what the consequence was-- a stick on your back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and I were together. My mother and the rest of the family, because they were all smaller, younger than I was at the time, they were at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- do you remember what you said to your family when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was hardly enough time to-- to say goodbye. And we always hoped that this is not the last time we're going to see each other. And the-- it was more of a goodbye, like, I'm going to see you soon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That day that we got all assembled in that-- that-- that public school that turned into stables later on, the men were all-- at night, when we were all finally assembled, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a Hungarian officer [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] came up and made us all line up like soldiers, like the army would line up. He counted-- he counted all of us. And he's berating us, telling us, you dirty Jews. The war is all your fault. We're in a war now that you-- you brought up on us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm going to make sure that you're going to stay here all night on your feet. And you're not going to be able to go in, back into the building. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just before he is leaving he says, I had a change of heart. I'm going to be nice to you. I'm going to let you go into the building and sleep, to go to sleep. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We all went into the building. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At the crack of dawn, as light started to-- the next day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somebody was saying, oh, my God, look at all our wives, and our sisters, and our brothers approaching the building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They brought everybody that they didn't take the day before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the younger siblings, the women, they brought them to the place that all the men were assembled the day before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of chaos started. They let us out of the building. Husbands ran to find their wives, to find their children. Children were looking for their mother and father. And children were hollering, daddy, mommy. But there were a lot of daddys and mommy calls. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether all families really got reunited again or not. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the way, the women and children were also told when they were told to assemble that they can carry everything-- just anything that they can carry on their back, no more. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People grabbed blankets. People grabbed food, whatever was important to-- to the individual. Some people, things were more important to others. Whatever they thought was important, they-- that's what they had as hand baggage. When we were all together, they made all the men go up front and all the women follow. And there-- we're being told to march. We don't know where, but we're being told to walk. When the-- the soldiers, the guards that were watching us, realized that were too many women and children that are not going to be able to make it, they're not going to be able to walk, they ordered the local gentile population that had horse and buggies to bring all the horse and buggies to us. And they loaded all the old people, the women, and the children, and the horse and buggies. And they followed us. The man were up front. The women were following behind us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a warm May day. And we were walking. It was a snake of people as far as the eye can see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were walking, but we don't know where. As we pass little villages on the way to wherever they're taking us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the local gentile population were standing on the street. And they were watching us like-- like you watch, these days, a circus go by. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As the day wore on and we were walking, our baggage got heavier and heavier. The-- the heat got to us. We perspired. And people started to drop their baggage just on the side of the road. The local population, at first, were kind of shy. They were-- they didn't know how to act. Our guards encouraged them, go pick it up. It's yours. Go take it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were like-- they were watching and followed us and picking up stuff, whatever, when we got tired enough to carrying that valise, that package, whatever we carried. When we threw it down, they picked it up. And no questions asked, it was theirs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At the end of the day, somebody says, we're going to the town called [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That town had a railroad-- railroad track, a railroad station. The reason we walked there is because where we were at our ghetto, there was no railroad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As we approached, we saw a long line of railroad cattle cars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we all got closer to the place, there were soldiers surrounding the-- the railroad-- all the railroad cars and us. And they rushed us to board those cars. They said, throw down whatever you have. The only thing you can take is your food that you have with you. You're not going to need anything else. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of chaos ensued to that point. Because men, husbands and wives, children in the walk got lost. A lot of-- a lot of chaos, again, like the scene that we had before. Husbands were calling their wives names. Children were looking for their parents. But they have rushed us so much to walk in. Luckily, my family, my immediate family, my mother and father and siblings, did get together. And we were together in the same car. But other people, children wound up in cars with total strangers. And, now, we're all in the cars. We're all loaded up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you in the car?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 100 to 120 people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're all-- we're in the car. Everybody is thirsty. Everybody is tired from the walk. It was very quiet in the car, except for both children and-- and adults, you would hear, subdued cries from people. But it was-- we were tired. And it was at night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And suddenly, we hear the locomotive starting, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we're moving. We don't know where we're moving, but the train started to move. We're on the-- we were on the train for a couple of days. We looked through the-- the crevice of the-- of the railroad-- of the cattle car. And we see a town called Kaschau. We are in Hungary at this point. The train stops. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're surrounded with guards. Nobody gets off the train. But those labor battalions that I was mentioning before-- when they took the guys of military age and they gave them yellow arm bands-- they let them bring us water. As they let them bring us water, they opened the car to give us the water. Some of these guys recognizes a sister. Some of these guys recognizes a sweetheart, a mother, a father. But there's nothing they cannot-- there's nothing that they can do. They gave us water. They had to walk away from us. The train was continuing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As the train goes for another several days, the stench is terrible. There was no-- was no-- no wheres to go to a bathroom. There was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the adults sort of do everything they can to wait until dark sets in, because they-- there was no-- no wheres to-- no sanitary facilities. There was-- it was-- the stench was getting worse from day to day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there holes made in?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After several days, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somebody looked through, again, those crevices. And they see we're passing a railroad station. And the name is Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- it didn't mean anything to me. I was a little boy. I had never heard of this town-- of this city before. After we passed Krakow, not too long after, and they see the-- the German-- by the way, in Kaschau, when we stopped in Kaschau, the Hungarian army pulled back. And the Germans took over. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After we passed Krakow-- I cannot remember how long after that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They always timed the arrivals of these people like us into Auschwitz at night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People are more confused. For whatever reason they had, we-- all these transports came at night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's in the middle of the night. The train stops. At this point, we never heard of Auschwitz, Birkenau. We don't know where we are. Soon as the train stop, we hear what sounded like rifle butts hitting on the sides of the cars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The doors are being opened up. We see the SS. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the orders, they hollered at us. [NON-ENGLISH], the dirty Jew. [NON-ENGLISH], get out. Disembark. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we-- we were all confused. We didn't know what was happening. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to jump off the train. There was no-- no way of-- of walking down the step from the railroad car this high up of the ground. We had to jump off that train. Soon as we jump off the train, we-- there is confusion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People lose each other. The-- the SS had German shepherd police dogs next to them. The dogs are barking.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue at the next tape. One moment, Mr. Stern.
